task: I’m trying to set up a better review process for a few recurring edits so I stop making avoidable mistakes late in the day. Can you help me sketch a simple routine for checking the work, deciding when to pause and get a second look, and keeping a backup in case I need to roll back?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it simple and repeatable.

## Simple review routine

### 1) Do a first pass on your own
Before asking anyone else:
- watch the cut once without stopping
- check picture, sound, text, and timing
- note anything that feels off

### 2) Pause when the work changes shape
Get a second look when:
- a new revision changes the structure
- you’ve made a major trim or rework
- something technical changed, like relink or export settings
- you’re too deep in it to judge cleanly

### 3) Keep a backup before risky changes
Before anything that could break the cut:
- duplicate the project or sequence
- save a milestone version
- keep the last known good export if you have one

### 4) Review against a short checklist
Check:
- story / structure
- sync
- titles / legal
- audio levels
- export settings
- any temp elements still in the cut

### 5) Roll back if needed
If a change makes things worse:
- go back to the last clean save
- don’t try to patch a bad version endlessly
- keep the earlier version until the new one is confirmed

## Simple rule
- **Self-check first**
- **Second look after major changes**
- **Backup before risky moves**
- **Roll back to the last clean version if needed**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page review checklist** with a “pause / backup / roll back” flow.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is basically the right shape. But it’s still a little generic.

I’d want it tighter, like something I can actually run at 9:30 p.m. when I’m fried.

What I need added:
- a hard cutoff for when to stop trusting my own judgment
- a quick QC pass order, so I’m not rewatching the whole thing every time
- a simple versioning rule, like what counts as a milestone vs just a save
- where the backup lives, because local-only isn’t enough if a drive decides to be stupid

Also, for the “second look” part — I’d separate **creative uncertainty** from **technical risk**. Sometimes I just need fresh eyes on pacing or structure, but other times it’s because I touched relinks, frame rate, or export settings and I don’t want to find out at delivery.

If you want, tighten this into a real checklist with those two buckets and a version naming rule. That’d be more useful.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, night-shift-friendly version.

## Simple review routine

### 1) First pass: self-check
- Watch once straight through
- Check picture, sound, text, and timing
- Note anything that feels off

### 2) Know when to stop trusting your own read
Pause and get a second look if:
- you’ve watched it too many times
- the cut has changed shape
- you’re tired enough to start guessing
- the same section keeps feeling “fine” without actually feeling right

### 3) Separate the reason for the second look
**Creative uncertainty**
- pacing feels off
- structure is unclear
- a beat isn’t landing
- you’re not sure what to trim

**Technical risk**
- relink
- frame rate
- export settings
- audio mapping
- anything that could break the file

### 4) Quick QC pass order
Don’t rewatch everything every time. Check in this order:
1. beginning
2. problem section
3. end card / CTA
4. audio
5. export spot-check

### 5) Version rule
- **Save** = normal working save
- **Milestone** = before major changes, risky fixes, or handoff
- **Last known good** = the version you can safely roll back to

### 6) Backup rule
Keep backups in two places:
- **local** working backup
- **off-site/cloud** copy of the milestone or last known good version

### 7) Roll back rule
If the new version gets worse:
- go back to the last clean milestone
- don’t keep patching the broken version
- keep the earlier file until the new one is confirmed

## Simple rule
- **Creative doubt** → second look
- **Technical risk** → backup first
- **Bad version** → roll back fast

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page late-night checklist** with checkbox formatting.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still a little too abstract for how I’d actually use it at night.

What I’d want now is the checklist in a more operational form — like:
- a hard stop threshold for fatigue, not just “you’re tired enough”
- exact pass order for QC that starts with the highest-risk stuff
- a naming convention I can actually slap on files without thinking
- a backup rule that says where the off-site copy lives and what gets copied every time

Also, the second-look split is right, but I’d want the trigger conditions spelled out a bit more. For example, if I touch relinks/frame rate/export, that’s automatic technical review. If I’m just unsure about pacing, that’s creative review, and I don’t need the same level of backup overhead.

So yeah — same direction, but I want the actual checklist version now. No fluff, just the thing I can follow at 9:30 p.m. when I’m cooked.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **actual checklist version**.

## Late-night review checklist

### 1) Hard stop on self-trust
Stop trusting your own judgment if:
- you’ve watched the same section 3 times
- you’re making the same note twice
- you’re too tired to tell whether something is actually wrong
- you start saying “it’s probably fine”

If any of those happen, pause and get a second look.

---

### 2) Decide which kind of review this is
**Creative review**
- pacing feels off
- structure feels weak
- a section isn’t landing
- you’re unsure what to trim

**Technical review**
- relink changed
- frame rate changed
- export settings changed
- audio mapping changed
- anything could break in delivery

If it’s technical, treat it as higher risk.

---

### 3) QC pass order
Check in this order:
1. **opening**
2. **problem section**
3. **titles / legal / end card**
4. **audio**
5. **export spot-check**

Don’t rewatch the whole thing unless you need to.

---

### 4) Versioning rule
Use three labels only:

- **save** = normal working save
- **milestone** = before risky changes or handoff
- **last_good** = the version you can roll back to

### File naming
- `ProjectName_v012_save`
- `ProjectName_v012_milestone`
- `ProjectName_v012_last_good`

Bump the version when the cut changes materially.

---

### 5) Backup rule
Keep:
- **local copy** on your working drive
- **off-site copy** in cloud or a second drive stored elsewhere

### Every time you make a milestone:
Copy:
- project file
- notes / change log
- current export
- anything needed to reopen the cut

### Don’t copy:
- cache
- previews
- scratch renders
- anything rebuildable

---

### 6) When to trigger second look
**Creative second look**
- pacing is uncertain
- structure changed
- you’re not sure the cut is landing

**Technical second look**
- relink touched
- frame rate changed
- export settings changed
- audio mapping changed

If it’s technical, get a second look before delivery.

---

### 7) Rollback rule
If the new version gets worse:
- go back to `last_good`
- don’t keep patching the broken version
- keep the earlier file until the new one is confirmed

## Simple rule
- **Creative doubt** → second look
- **Technical change** → milestone + backup
- **Bad version** → roll back fast

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checkbox sheet** with the file names and backup steps laid out cleanly.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, closer. But that naming thing is still a little too loose for how I’d actually use it.

I’d want:
- one fixed version bump rule, not just “materially”
- a clearer cutoff for when a save becomes a milestone
- the off-site copy spelled out as either cloud or a specific second physical location, not both as an option every time
- and the QC order tightened so technical risk gets checked before I waste time on creative polish

Also, if we’re keeping the two-bucket split, I’d want the technical side to explicitly force a backup + rollback point before I touch anything. That’s the part that matters when I’m cooked.

So yeah — same shape, but I’d want the checklist version with the exact file naming and backup path written out cleanly, not just described.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter checklist version.

## Late-night review checklist

### 1) Hard stop on self-trust
Stop trusting your own judgment if:
- you’ve watched the same section 3 times
- you’re making the same note twice
- you start saying “probably fine”
- you’re too tired to tell whether it’s actually wrong

If any of those happen, pause and get a second look.

---

### 2) Decide which kind of review this is
**Creative review**
- pacing feels off
- structure changed
- a section isn’t landing
- you’re unsure what to trim

**Technical review**
- relink changed
- frame rate changed
- export settings changed
- audio mapping changed
- anything could break in delivery

If it’s technical, stop and back up before touching anything else.

---

### 3) QC pass order
Check in this order:
1. **technical risk first** — relink, frame rate, export settings, audio mapping
2. **opening**
3. **problem section**
4. **titles / legal / end card**
5. **audio**
6. **export spot-check**

Don’t spend time polishing until technical risk is clear.

---

### 4) Versioning rule
Use one fixed rule:

- **save** = normal working save
- **milestone** = any time you make a technical change or finish a meaningful revision
- **last_good** = the version you can safely roll back to

### File naming
- `ProjectName_v012_save`
- `ProjectName_v012_milestone`
- `ProjectName_v012_last_good`

### Bump the version when:
- the cut changes structurally
- a technical change is made
- the version is going to review or handoff

---

### 5) Backup rule
Pick one off-site path and keep it consistent:
- **cloud** or
- **second physical location**

### Every milestone copy should include:
- project file
- notes / change log
- current export
- anything needed to reopen the cut

### Do not back up:
- cache
- previews
- scratch renders
- anything rebuildable

---

### 6) Technical change rule
If you touch:
- relinks
- frame rate
- export settings
- audio mapping

Then you must:
1. make a milestone save
2. copy it to the off-site backup path
3. keep the last_good version untouched
4. review the technical risk before moving on

---

### 7) Creative review rule
If it’s only:
- pacing
- structure
- whether a beat lands
- what to trim

Then:
- get a second look if needed
- don’t force a full backup cycle unless the cut changed materially

---

### 8) Rollback rule
If the new version gets worse:
- go back to `last_good`
- don’t keep patching the broken version
- keep the earlier file until the new one is confirmed

## Simple rule
- **Creative doubt** → second look
- **Technical change** → milestone + backup first
- **Bad version** → roll back fast

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checkbox sheet** with the file names and backup path filled in.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. Better, but still not quite locked.

I’d want the version rule to be one step tighter, like:
- every structural change = next version
- every technical change = next version + milestone
- every handoff/review export = next version + last_good

And for the hard stop, I’d rather it be something I can actually feel in the room:
- same section 2 times and I’m still not sure, or
- I’m rereading notes because I can’t hold the cut in my head, or
- I start making judgment calls based on fatigue instead of what I’m seeing

That’s the point where I stop pretending I’m still sharp.

Also, the backup path needs to be more concrete. For me it’d be:
- working copy on the local edit drive
- milestone copy to cloud or the NAS at home, not both
- if it’s technical, I duplicate before the change and leave the pre-change version alone until the new one passes

So yeah — I’m basically asking for the final pass on this as a real checklist, with the version bump logic and backup destination written as hard rules, not options. That’s the part I’d actually use at night.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the final, hard-rule version.

## Late-night review checklist

### 1) Hard stop on self-trust
Stop and get a second look if:
- you’ve watched the same section **2 times** and still aren’t sure
- you’re rereading notes because you can’t hold the cut in your head
- you start making calls based on fatigue instead of what you’re seeing

If that happens, don’t keep pushing.

---

### 2) Version bump rules
Use one fixed rule:

- **Every structural change = next version**
- **Every technical change = next version + milestone**
- **Every handoff/review export = next version + last_good**

### Example
- `ProjectName_v012_work`
- `ProjectName_v013_milestone`
- `ProjectName_v013_last_good`
- `ProjectName_v014_review`
- `ProjectName_v015_final`

---

### 3) Backup destination
Use:
- **working copy** = local edit drive
- **milestone copy** = cloud **or** home NAS
- not both unless you specifically choose to mirror both

### Hard rule
If it’s technical, duplicate before the change and leave the pre-change version alone until the new one passes.

---

### 4) What counts as a technical change
Treat these as technical:
- relinks
- frame rate
- export settings
- audio mapping

If you touch any of those:
1. make a milestone save
2. copy it to the off-site path
3. keep `last_good` untouched
4. review the technical risk before moving on

---

### 5) QC pass order
Check in this order:
1. technical risk first — relink, frame rate, export settings, audio mapping
2. opening
3. problem section
4. titles / legal / end card
5. audio
6. export spot-check

Don’t spend time polishing until technical risk is clear.

---

### 6) Creative review rule
If it’s only:
- pacing
- structure
- whether a beat lands
- what to trim

Then:
- get a second look if needed
- don’t force a full backup cycle unless the cut changed structurally

---

### 7) Rollback rule
If the new version gets worse:
- go back to `last_good`
- don’t keep patching the broken version
- keep the earlier file until the new one is confirmed

## Simple rule
- **Creative doubt** → second look
- **Technical change** → next version + milestone backup first
- **Bad version** → roll back fast

If you want, I can turn this into a **single-page checkbox sheet** with the filenames and backup path already filled in.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the usable version.

The versioning rule is finally clean enough to run without thinking about it, and the hard-stop threshold is something I’d actually notice at the end of the night. The technical bucket forcing a pre-change duplicate is the part I wanted most.

I’d probably use it like this:
- structural change = next version
- technical change = next version + milestone + pre-change duplicate
- handoff/review export = next version + last_good

And for backup, local working copy plus one off-site target, period. Cloud or NAS, not both unless I decide to mirror intentionally.

This is close enough to drop into my workflow as-is.
